data.title: Avista raises dividend by 0.5% to $0.4925 a share

  • Forward yield 4.68%
  • Payable March 13; for shareholders of record Feb. 25; ex-div Feb. 25.

  • See AVA Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Trump orders U.S. government to prepare release of UFO, alien files
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Thursday said he is directing the Defense Department and other agencies to “begin the process of identifying and releasing Government files related to alien and extraterrestrial life, unidentified aerial phenomena (UAP), and unidentified flying objects (UFOs).”

He made the declaration in a post on Truth Social, after he accused former President Barack Obama earlier in the day of revealing classified information during public remarks about aliens, saying he “made a big mistake.”

"He took it out of classified information ... He's not supposed to be doing that," Trump told reporters while traveling to Georgia. Trump added that he had not seen evidence that aliens exist, saying, "I don't know if they're real or not."

During an interview with podcast host Brian Tyler Cohen released on Saturday, Obama was asked if aliens were real.

"They're real, but I haven't seen them, and they're not being kept in ... Area 51. There's no underground facility unless there's this enormous conspiracy and they hid it from the president of the United States," Obama said.

In recent years, the Pentagon has examined reports of UFOs, and senior military officials said in 2022 they found no evidence that extraterrestrials had visited or crash-landed on Earth.

data.title: LegalZoom outlines 8% revenue growth target for 2026 while expanding human-in-the-loop strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: LegalZoom.com, Inc. (LZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey Stibel emphasized LegalZoom's 25-year journey and its strategic pivot toward integrating AI and human expertise, stating, "LegalZoom makes it safe and seamless to finish. Since I became CEO, we have been steadily refocusing our business to capture the AI opportunity while recognizing that certain tasks and complex legal matters will always require human judgment and supervision." He highlighted the company's "human-in-the-loop" approach as the driver of growth, combining automation with expert intervention.
  • Stibel explained that the strategy is to "be the trusted guardians of small businesses and individuals lives and aspirations, enabled by the best technology available," focusing on automating where possible and leveraging human expertise for nuanced tasks. He announced the expansion beyond business formations to serve existing businesses, aiming to increase share of their serviceable addressable market through expert and service layers, including legal advice subscriptions, trademark and IP services, and concierge offerings.
  • CFO and COO Noel Watson stated, "For the full year 2025, we grew revenue 11% to $756 million, more than double the growth rate from our initial outlook, inclusive of the Formation Nation acquisition." He added, "Full year subscription revenue increased 13%... and we expect that momentum to continue in 2026."
  • Watson also highlighted a focus on higher-value subscriptions and AI-enabled services, noting, "Our strategy is focused on building a more resilient revenue base through higher value subscription offerings and AI-enabled human-in-the-loop services."

Outlook

  • LegalZoom projects full year 2026 revenue in the range of $805 million to $825 million, which Watson described as "approximately 8% year-over-year growth at the midpoint." The acceleration is expected to come from higher value offerings, quality customer acquisition, and continued execution of the human-in-the-loop strategy.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for 2026 is expected between $190 million and $200 million. Watson said, "Our outlook reflects improved gross margins and disciplined cost management, partially offset by higher product and marketing investments."
  • For Q1 2026, revenue is expected in the range of $200 million to $203 million with adjusted EBITDA of $34 million to $36 million, reflecting a temporary year-over-year decline due to front-loaded marketing investments.
  • Management stated that a recent 5% reduction in headcount was completed "allowing for improved operating leverage while preserving investment in high-growth initiatives."

Financial Results

  • LegalZoom reported Q4 2025 total revenue of $190 million, with subscription revenue reaching $131 million, marking the fourth consecutive quarter of accelerating growth.
  • Subscription units ended at approximately 1.94 million, up 10% year-over-year, with ARPU at $266 for the quarter.
  • Transaction revenue for Q4 was $59 million, with average order value at $248, up 13% year-over-year. The company processed 112,000 business formations, a 17% increase.
  • Deferred revenue declined by $10 million sequentially, attributed to normal seasonality.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $50 million, representing a margin of 26%. Free cash flow for the quarter was $28 million, with $148 million for the full year.
  • Cash and cash equivalents stood at $203 million, with share repurchases totaling approximately $80 million for the year. The Board approved a $100 million increase to the share repurchase authorization.

Q&A

  • Eleanor Smith, JPMorgan: Asked about early metrics on the concierge product and its impact on 2026 expectations. Stibel responded that "there are some early proof points and the green shoots, and we have factored those in... We're still in the early innings."
  • Smith followed up on customer acquisition marketing. Stibel indicated increased Q1 marketing spend to target high-value customers, with Watson adding, "The Q1 incremental marketing spend is driven primarily by brand... we expect CAM spend to be relatively in line from a percent of revenue standpoint for the full year."
  • Trevor Young, Barclays: Inquired about revenue growth cadence and what needs to happen for durable double-digit growth. Watson noted tougher comps due to prior year initiatives, while Stibel pointed to expanding the human-in-the-loop strategy and market penetration as essential for future acceleration.
  • Michael McGovern, BofA: Asked about the mechanics of LegalZoom's role as a last-mile provider for LLMs. Stibel said, "The bottom line is the right answer is we don't know and they don't know yet. However, both parties have identified a problem... And we want to be that solution to the now what."
  • Lucas Cerisola, Morgan Stanley: Requested details on Formation Nation's contribution in Q4. Watson confirmed, "Formation Nation contributed about $9.8 million on the transaction side and $5.7 million in subscription revenue."
  • Additional analyst questions focused on investments in human-in-the-loop services, top-of-funnel metrics, competitive dynamics, and marketing strategy, with management reiterating their emphasis on quality share, efficiency, and leveraging AI.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions about growth durability, the pace of innovation, and the impact of AI partnerships, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with a focus on execution and strategy validation.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, frequently referencing "confidence," "momentum," and "strong execution," and using phrases like, "We are confident this will enable us to capture a greater share of our serviceable addressable market."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained optimistic but acknowledged the need for continued execution, while analysts continued to probe for specifics on strategy and growth sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 reflects an 8% revenue growth target, up from 3% organic growth in 2025, signaling increased confidence in higher-value offerings and the human-in-the-loop approach.
  • The previous quarter highlighted new partnerships and organizational realignment, while the current quarter emphasized operational execution, margin expansion, and active share repurchases.
  • Analyst questions in both quarters focused on the evolution of subscription models, the impact of AI, and the company's ability to sustain growth amid shifting market dynamics.
  • Management's confidence in the human-in-the-loop strategy and expanding addressable market has grown, with increased emphasis on leveraging AI for both internal efficiency and customer acquisition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited normal seasonality affecting deferred revenue and the need to balance cost discipline with ongoing investments in marketing and product.
  • Watson noted, "We continue to be disciplined with head count as our organization onboards more AI and technology into our processes."
  • Analysts voiced concerns about the pace of customer acquisition, competitive intensity, and the execution of new product rollouts, while management stressed their focus on quality share and margin improvement.

Final Takeaway

LegalZoom enters 2026 with a strengthened foundation, targeting 8% revenue growth and positioning its human-in-the-loop services and high-value subscriptions as primary engines for durable expansion. Management continues to leverage AI-driven efficiencies while prioritizing quality customer acquisition and operational discipline, supported by robust free cash flow and an expanded share repurchase authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nvidia and OpenAI abandon unfinished $100B deal in favour of $30B investment, FT reports
data.description:
• U.S. chip giant Nvidia (NVDA) is close to finalizing a $30 billion investment into OpenAI (OPENAI) that will replace the long-term $100 billion commitment agreed upon by the companies last year, the Financial Times reported on Thursday.
  • The $30B equity investment is part of a funding round that is on track to raise more than $100B and will value OpenAI at $730B, not including the new money, the report said.
  • OpenAI will invest much of the new capital in Nvidia hardware, and the deal could be finalized as early as this weekend, the report added.

data.title: Texas Roadhouse outlines 1.9% menu price increase and targets 35 new company-owned openings for 2026 amid sustained sales growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gerald Morgan highlighted that "2025 was another successful year as revenue grew to nearly $5.9 billion, and all 3 brands delivered positive sales and traffic growth." The company celebrated its 800th system-wide restaurant opening and the acquisition of 20 franchise locations. Over 70% of restaurants set both daily and weekly sales records, the digital kitchen and guest management systems rollout was completed, and the support center buildings in Louisville were purchased.

  • Morgan stated, "In 2025, we added 48 restaurants to our company-owned restaurant base... and our franchise partners opened 4 restaurants, including 3 international Texas Roadhouses and 1 domestic Jaggers." For 2026, the company expects "approximately 35 company restaurant openings across the 3 brands" and confirmed the acquisition of 5 California franchise restaurants at the start of the fiscal year.

  • The company plans to implement a 1.9% menu price increase at the beginning of Q2 2026 and will focus on beverage offerings, including mocktails, dirty sodas, and a $5 all-day beverage special.

  • CFO Mike Lenihan introduced himself, expressing, "I'm honored to have the privilege of joining Texas Roadhouse... I've immersed myself into the culture of the support center, learning about the incredible hard work, people-first approach and teamwork needed to support our restaurants."

  • Keith Humpich, Chief Accounting & Financial Services Officer, noted, "Same-store sales increased 4.9% for the full year, including 2.8% traffic growth. Consolidated average unit volume exceeded $8.4 million with average weekly sales of over $166,000 at Texas Roadhouse, $122,000 at Bubba's 33 and nearly $73,000 at Jaggers." He also reported, "We still generated the second highest restaurant margin dollars, income from operations and earnings per share in our history."

Outlook

  • Humpich stated, "Our commodity inflation guidance of approximately 7% remains unchanged with the continued expectation of being above the guidance in the first half of the year and below the guidance in the second half of the year. Beef inflation accounts for nearly all of the expected commodity inflation throughout the year."

  • Wage and other labor inflation guidance remains at 3% to 4%, with the wage component expected to moderate despite state-mandated increases. The company maintains a capital expenditure guidance of approximately $400 million, excluding the $72 million paid for the California franchise acquisition.

  • The forecast for the full year 2026 income tax rate was updated to between 14% and 15%.

Financial Results

  • Bailen reported, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, we reported revenue growth of 3.1%, driven by a 4% increase in average weekly sales, partially offset by a 0.6% decline in store weeks." Restaurant margin dollars decreased 15.6% to $205 million, and diluted earnings per share decreased 26.1% to $1.28. Average weekly sales in Q4 were over $160,000, with to-go representing approximately $22,000 or 13.8% of these sales.

  • Comparable sales increased 4.2% in Q4, with 1.9% traffic growth and a 2.3% increase in average check. Restaurant margin as a percentage of total sales declined 309 basis points year-over-year to 13.9%. Food and beverage costs as a percentage of total sales were 36.4% for Q4, reflecting 9.5% commodity inflation. Labor as a percentage of total sales increased 18 basis points to 33.2% compared to the previous year.

  • Cash flow from operations for the full year was over $730 million, with $388 million in capital expenditures and $180 million returned to shareholders through dividends and $150 million in share repurchases.

Q&A

  • David Palmer, Evercore ISI, asked about the December sales slowdown and beef inflation longevity. Michael Bailen responded that the labor hours ratio spike "feels like it was a little bit of an anomaly" due to holiday shifts and weather, and that the company will focus on top line growth to leverage labor and other costs.

  • Andrew Charles, TD Cowen, asked about the digital kitchen's impact. Bailen said, "It has led to that calmer, quieter restaurant -- excuse me, kitchen experience. And I think it does free us up to do more to-go business."

  • Sara Senatore, BofA Securities, inquired about pricing. Bailen replied, "We had 3.1% pricing for the fourth quarter. We'll have that same 3.1% here in the first quarter. And then with the 1.9% rolling on, that means we'll have 3.6% in the menu for the second and third quarters."

  • Jeffrey Farmer, Gordon Haskett, asked about restaurant margin for 2026. Bailen noted the challenge in cost of sales leverage with 7% commodity inflation, but opportunities in other components of restaurant margin.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions were probing, focusing on margin pressures, the impact of weather, inflation, and technology initiatives. The tone was neutral to slightly cautious, particularly regarding inflationary headwinds and cost pressures.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing long-term growth and stability. In the Q&A, responses were measured and sometimes defensive, particularly when discussing margin pressures and inflation: "We are confident in our long-term strategy and believe we are set up for continued success over the coming years," (Humpich).

  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment was more cautious, with more analyst focus on margin headwinds and inflation. Management’s confidence was steady, though more defensive language was present in Q&A.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company’s guidance for commodity inflation, wage and labor inflation, and capital expenditures remained consistent with the previous quarter.

  • Management reiterated its strategy of conservative pricing and focus on value. The Q4 transcript revealed increased analyst attention to the impact of weather, inflation, and technology rollout, versus the Q3 focus on expansion and consumer dynamics.

  • Key metrics such as same-store sales, average weekly sales, and cash flow were highlighted in both quarters, with Q4 showing a more pronounced impact from commodity inflation and a sequential drop in restaurant margin percentage.

  • The tone of management remained confident, but Q4 featured more defensive explanations regarding cost pressures, while analysts expressed slightly increased caution compared to Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified commodity inflation, especially beef, as a continuing headwind for 2026. Wage and other labor inflation, as well as utility and insurance costs, are also expected to pressure margins.

  • Analysts raised concerns about weather-driven sales impacts, the durability of share gains, and the effectiveness of technology investments.

  • Management’s mitigation efforts include menu price increases, technology integration for efficiency, and focus on top line growth to offset cost pressures.

Final Takeaway

Texas Roadhouse closed 2025 with robust revenue growth, record-setting sales at a majority of locations, and continued expansion across all brands. The company is navigating commodity and labor inflation by implementing a 1.9% menu price increase in Q2 2026 and investing in technology to enhance operational efficiency. With 35 new company-owned openings planned and ongoing franchise acquisitions, management remains committed to driving top line growth, maintaining value for guests, and delivering returns to shareholders despite ongoing cost challenges.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Universal Display outlines $650M-$700M revenue target for 2026 while expanding blue OLED innovation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Universal Display Corporation (OLED) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Steven V. Abramson, President and CEO, announced "record 2025 revenue of $651 million. Operating income was $249 million and net income was $242 million or $5.08 per diluted share." He emphasized strong execution and expanding OLED adoption across consumer electronics, highlighting continued R&D investment, global infrastructure expansion, and deepened customer engagement.
  • Abramson described the OLED industry's evolution, stating "the OLED industry is entering a new phase, marked by broader applications, higher performance expectations and a more diverse set of device architectures." He referenced Universal Display's ongoing leadership, intellectual property acquisition from Merck KGaA, and the company's robust pipeline across red, green, and blue emissive materials.
  • The CEO focused on the company's advancements in phosphorescent blue, noting "our confidence in blue remains unwavering. Breakthroughs of this magnitude are rarely linear. Once adopted, we believe our phosphorescent blue can enable up to a 25% improvement in OLED panel energy efficiency."
  • Abramson detailed market opportunities, citing Omdia data projecting global OLED shipments to surpass 1.4 billion units by 2030, with significant growth in IT, automotive, and foldables. He noted, "2026 marks a significant industry milestone with the world's first Gen 8.6 OLED facilities, Samsung Display in Korea and BOE in China entering mass production."
  • Brian Millard, CFO, stated, "2025 ended on a strong note with record fourth quarter and annual revenues that were in line with our expectations from November." He reported material sales of $353 million, royalty and license revenues of $275 million, total gross margin of 76%, and an operating margin of 38%. Millard also highlighted a cumulative catch-up adjustment of $14 million for the year.
  • The CFO discussed share repurchases, stating "we have repurchased approximately 454,000 shares of common stock for $53 million," and announced an increase in the quarterly cash dividend to $0.50 per share.

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 revenues in the range of $650 million to $700 million. Millard provided, "We estimate that our ratio of materials to royalty and licensing revenues will be in the ballpark of 1.3:1. Total gross margins are expected to be approximately in the range of 74% to 76% as a result of higher raw material pricing."
  • R&D and SG&A expenses are anticipated to grow in the mid- to high single-digit percentage year-over-year, with operating margins projected in the range of 34% to 37% and an effective tax rate of approximately 19%.
  • Millard stated, "the dividend increase reflects the confidence in our robust future growth opportunities, expected continued positive cash flow generation and commitment to return capital to our shareholders."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter 2025 revenue was $173 million, up from $162 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Material sales reached $96 million, with green emitter sales at $74 million and red emitter sales at $21 million.
  • Royalty and license fees for the quarter were $73 million.
  • Adesis revenue for the quarter was $4.8 million.
  • Cost of sales in the fourth quarter was $41 million, resulting in a gross margin of 76%. Operating expenses excluding cost of sales were $64 million.
  • Operating income for the fourth quarter was $67 million, with net income at $66 million or $1.39 per diluted share.
  • The company ended the year with $955 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments.

Q&A

  • Brian Lee, Goldman Sachs, asked about bottlenecks to blue and visibility to updates in 2026. Millard answered, "much of the progress here forward is in the customers' hands in terms of getting it into a commercial device for the market, while our work continues in terms of developing and inventing new materials."
  • Lee inquired about developmental revenues for blue, noting a decline. Millard responded, "it's still going to be developmental in nature. So I think modeling kind of around the zone that we've been for the last few years is a reasonable place to go."
  • Lee questioned inventory trends in China and catch-up payments. Millard said, "most of that tariff buying has kind of worked its way through as we exit '25. And on seasonality for this year, we are expecting more of a return to our historical pattern, which is the second half being stronger than the first half."
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham, asked about new Gen 8.6 lines and capacity builds. Millard explained, "Samsung's fab is not expected to come online till sometime in Q2 and BOE's shortly thereafter...they are incorporated in our overall guidance for the year and to some degree, are weighing on that second half orientation."
  • Ricchiuti inquired about competition in China. Millard stated, "China remains a critical market and a growing market for us and for the industry, and we're continuing to support our customers in China...we are making additional investment in the Chinese market."
  • Ricchiuti asked for updates on LG contract talks. Millard responded, "their contract did expire at the end of last year. We're working through the details of a new contract with them. So nothing to announce today, but things are progressing as we'd expect."
  • Mehdi Hosseini, SIG, asked about the revenue guide's underlying assumptions. Millard answered, "the guidance at the midpoint and overall really is in line with the industry growth that's projected in terms of area...there are concerns about memory pricing and availability this year that are factoring in on the downside."
  • Martin Yang, Oppenheimer, questioned the cumulative catch-up figure. Millard clarified, "the cumulative catch-up was across all of our customers...it's not end market specific."
  • Woo Jin Ho, Bloomberg Intelligence, asked about declining gross margins. Millard noted, "part of what's caused gross margin over the last year to decrease modestly is volume pricing... there has been a slight decrease in ASP just as volume and scale has increased in the industry."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts repeatedly raised concerns about blue commercialization timelines, competitive pressures in China, gross margin declines, and the impact of new capacity additions, reflecting a neutral to slightly negative tone with persistent questioning over visibility and profitability.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing ongoing innovation, robust customer engagement, and their leadership position. Millard's remarks included "the path that we're on and the enthusiasm we have for the product continues to be very strong." However, the tone showed more caution and detail on headwinds compared to the previous quarter.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst skepticism over blue revenue trajectory and margin headwinds increased, while management shifted from highlighting innovation and growth to a more measured discussion of competitive risks and cost pressures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's outlook maintained the same revenue range as the previous quarter, but now includes more explicit discussion of margin pressures and cost drivers.
  • Strategic commentary shifted toward emphasizing the importance of blue and the complexity of OLED architectures, compared to the prior focus on patent acquisition and capacity expansion.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on granular financial issues such as catch-up payments, gross margin headwinds, and competitive pressures, versus the broader capacity and growth questions of the previous call.
  • Management's tone became more cautious, specifically addressing rising raw material costs and competitive dynamics, whereas the previous call centered more on opportunities and innovation.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited higher raw material costs, particularly iridium, as a primary driver for lower gross margin guidance for 2026.
  • Competitive pressures in China were discussed, with management noting increased competition and efforts to bolster local support and patent defense.
  • Uncertainty around the pace of blue commercialization was acknowledged, with developmental revenues expected to remain flat year-over-year.
  • Ongoing contract negotiations with LG were noted, with no immediate resolution but continued confidence expressed.
  • Analysts raised concerns about visibility into blue revenue growth, margin declines, inventory normalization in China, and competitive threats from local players.

Final Takeaway

Universal Display Corporation reported record 2025 results and set a 2026 revenue target between $650 million and $700 million, while guiding for slightly lower gross margins due to higher raw material costs and competitive pricing dynamics. Management expressed continued confidence in the commercial potential of blue OLED technology and highlighted expanding end-market opportunities, especially in IT and automotive. The company is focusing on operational agility and capital returns, while actively managing industry headwinds and competitive pressures to support long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: AMN Healthcare outlines $600M labor disruption revenue for Q1 2026 while targeting 4%-6% organic growth beyond 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AMN Healthcare Services, Inc. (AMN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Cary Grace, President, CEO & Director, highlighted "outsized labor disruption revenue in the fourth quarter and with 2 large events in the first quarter, we anticipate significantly more labor disruption revenue this quarter." She reported full-year 2025 revenue of $2.73 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $234 million, with debt reduced by $285 million in 2025. "Fourth quarter revenue of $748 million was 2% higher than the year ago quarter and $18 million above the high end of guidance."
  • Grace noted, "Nurse and Allied revenue of $491 million grew 8% year-over-year. Excluding labor disruption, segment revenue was down 7% year-over-year, improved from down 13% in the third quarter." She also emphasized investments in AI technology enablement, stating, "We are expanding our capabilities by investing in AI technology enablement to support the administrative and other nonclinical interactions with patients where human interaction is not required."
  • Brian Scott, CFO & COO, stated, "Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $748 million, above the high end of our guidance range, driven by labor disruption revenue that was $24 million above guidance." He added, "Gross margin declined 370 basis points year-over-year and 300 basis points sequentially. Labor disruption revenue reduced fourth quarter consolidated gross margin by 130 basis points."

Outlook

  • The company projects consolidated revenue for Q1 2026 to be in the range of $1.225 billion to $1.24 billion, including approximately $600 million related to labor disruption support. Gross margin is projected to be between 23.5% and 24%, with labor disruption revenue expected to reduce gross margin by about 300 basis points. Scott stated, "Operating margin is expected to be 5.9% to 6.5% and adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to be 9.7% to 10.2%."
  • Grace indicated, "After 2026 and excluding labor disruption, we see a path to delivering sustainable organic revenue growth of 4% to 6% per year while growing operating expenses at half the rate of revenue growth, resulting in 10% to 15% growth of adjusted EBITDA."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $748 million, an 18% sequential increase. Adjusted EBITDA was $54 million, down 27% year-over-year and 5% sequentially. Adjusted EBITDA margin for the quarter was 7.3%.
  • Fourth quarter net loss was $8 million, compared to net loss of $188 million in the prior year period and net income of $29 million in the prior quarter. GAAP loss per share was ($0.20). Adjusted earnings per share was $0.22 compared with $0.75 in the prior year period and $0.39 in the prior quarter.
  • Days sales outstanding for the quarter was 47 days, 8 days lower than a year ago and 10 days lower sequentially. Operating cash flow was $76 million; capital expenditures were $8 million. The company ended the year with cash and equivalents of $34 million and total debt of $775 million, with a net leverage ratio of 3.3x to 1.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Silber, BMO: Asked about the operational separation of the labor disruption business. Grace explained, "We have developed over and invested in over the past 2 years, technology and operating model to be able to support strike events...We have a dedicated strike team...So we really have built a system so that we will have as little disruption to our core business as possible when we're supporting these types of events."
  • Silber followed up on AI risks in the language translation business. Grace responded, "Our language service business...is focused on clinical health care setting. And that by government regulation is required to have a human interface...We have been focused much more on the clinical side of it...We have had clients who are looking at the overall patient journey and really wanting to ensure continuity...So...we're investing in AI enablement...where you don't have that mandate and regulation."
  • Albert Rice, UBS: Queried on the supply of nurses for labor disruption. Grace said, "We have crisis workers that are known to us...We have had very, very high fill rates in both of these events."
  • Rice also asked about the Kaiser contract. Grace stated, "Our contract with Kaiser goes through the end of this year. We expect them...to go through an RFP this year."
  • Rice inquired about visa retrogression changes. Grace replied, "We expect mid-teens growth in 2026 from international. That's a higher-margin business for us."
  • Kevin Fischbeck, BofA: Asked if labor disruption crowds out core business. Grace clarified, "We have built our ability to support strike also enables us to continue to support our core clients...we've been able to do both simultaneously."
  • Fischbeck probed on pricing dynamics. Grace said, "If you have an order that's priced right, it gets filled...Outside of strike, you might have systems that can wait a week or two to see if an order gets filled and then they can increase price."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally inquisitive but expressed surprise at the scale of labor disruption revenue and probed for risks, with questions on operational separation, AI risks, supply sourcing, and contract exposure. The tone was neutral to slightly cautious, especially regarding the sustainability and impact of labor disruption on core operations.
  • Management adopted a confident tone in prepared remarks, stressing operational readiness and the strategic upside of recent investments. In the Q&A, responses were detailed, sometimes emphasizing regulatory protections or competitive advantages. Phrases like "we have built a system" and "we have great experiences with them" indicate confidence, while acknowledgment of market pressures and regulatory headwinds added nuance. Compared with the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive regarding operational capabilities and strategic progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance increased dramatically for Q1 2026 due to $600 million in anticipated labor disruption revenue, compared to $100 million in the previous quarter’s guidance. The underlying nurse and allied growth outlook improved from a year-over-year decline to flat-to-positive, excluding labor disruption.
  • Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism regarding demand stabilization in Q3 2025 to stronger confidence in operational execution, technology investments, and market positioning in Q4 2025.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from margin normalization and bill rate stabilization in Q3 to the impact and sustainability of labor disruption and potential AI-related risks in Q4.
  • Key metrics reflect a sequential improvement in revenue and significant reduction in days sales outstanding, with adjusted EBITDA margin pressured by the lower-margin labor disruption business.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that large-scale labor disruption events require reallocation of resources but stressed that processes are in place to minimize core business impact.
  • Analyst concerns focused on labor disruption’s effect on core operations, competitive pressures in language services, and potential regulatory or legislative changes affecting contingent labor and language services.
  • Grace addressed AI risks by highlighting regulatory safeguards in clinical language services and outlined proactive investments in AI for nonclinical areas as a response to competitive pressures.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored AMN Healthcare’s ability to manage unprecedented labor disruption events without undermining its core business, leveraging investments in technology, operational playbooks, and AI enablement. Looking ahead, the company expects a year of transition in 2026, supported by robust pipelines, growing demand in key segments, and a clear strategy to achieve sustainable 4% to 6% organic revenue growth and double-digit EBITDA growth beyond 2026, while maintaining flexibility and a strong balance sheet amid evolving market and regulatory dynamics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
